data.title: Nucor: Ignoring Short-Term Noise Is Important
data.description:
The U.S. Steel sector has come under the spotlight recently on the back of recently implemented tariffs on steel and aluminum products. Additionally, the U.S. has turned into a major growth market for steel, as more production is likely to be on-shored over the coming years.

As a result, in recent months, we have seen a significant increase in trading volumes of the four major U.S. steel producers – Nucor Corporation (NYSE:NUE), Steel Dynamics (NASDAQ:STLD), U.S. Steel (NYSE:X) and Cleveland-Cliffs (NYSE:CLF).

While for some investors these four names could be seen as interchangeable, the reality is that we see a very wide performance gap in recent years with some companies changing their strategies and other awaiting some important political decisions. Interestingly, Nucor is one of the most profitable enterprises within the peer group and yet, the stock's performance over the 5-year period leaves a lot to be desired.

Following these movements in recent years, NUE now trades at the lowest forward EV/EBITDA multiple within the peer group, while the company is also among the most profitable ones on an EBITDA basis.

On a historical basis, NUE also trades cheaply relative to its current EBITDA margin, with its current Price/Sales multiple of 0.8a already pricing in further margin downside in 2025.

This relative valuation indicates that there are some short-term imbalances within the U.S. steel sector which could create attractive long-term opportunities for investors. Especially, once we factor in the ongoing volatility in the equity market and the geopolitical risks that are manifesting through the ongoing trade war.

With that in mind, it is crucial that we take a long-term view on the sector and assess the attractiveness of NUE without being influenced by short-term noise.

Taking A Long-Term View

In terms of total revenue, Nucor has retained the leading position within the segment, with Cliffs currently occupying the second place on the back of its aggressive M&A strategy.

The big difference between Nucor's more organic growth led strategy and CLF's acquisition spree is that the former has managed to retain its high gross margins even during the currently challenging times for the industry. CLF, on the other hand, has taken a more risky approach to capacity expansion, which has taken its toll on profitability.

The different approach between CLF and NUE is also evident from the level of capital expenditure over the years and especially since 2022 when the gap between the two companies widened significantly. CLF continued to rely heavily on acquisitions and rescued its capex significantly, while NUE did exactly the opposite.

Nucor has also been active on the M&A front. However, the company has spent almost 5 times as much as CLF did in fiscal year 2024 on capital expenditure, which speaks volumes regarding the difference between the two companies' growth strategy.

As a result of this different approach, NUE has retained its industry-leading return on assets (ROTA) alongside Steel Dynamics. X and CLF, on the other hand, have had far less consistent ROTA over the years, which could result in sharp repricing opportunity in the near-future (especially in the case of X), but is a problem for long-term investors who are expecting more consistent returns over the years.

Not only that, but due to Nucor's more stable profitability the company has a leverage ratio (total debt to EBITDA) of only 1.6, which is the second lowest in the peer group after that of Steel Dynamics of 1.4. While none of the companies seem to have any immediate difficulties regarding their leverage, NUE shareholders are in a far better position to weather a more sustained downturn in the economy or steel demand and pricing.

Where Nucor Stands

As steel priced continued falling and in recent years and demand fluctuated, Nucor's cash flow from operations has fallen drastically in 2024 (see the graph below). Nonetheless, NUE's management did not cut its annual capex to cater to short-term investors and speculators. Quite the opposite, the company continues to invest more heavily in its operations and more downstream activities.

This long-term approach of NUE's management is highly likely to cause more pain in the short-run as management does not seem interested in managing the business on a quarter-by-quarter basis.

Nucor is also well-positioned from an end-market point of view. Roughly 50% of sales come from construction & infrastructure. While residential construction is highly cyclical, data centers and large infrastructure projects are less exposed to changes in the business cycle.

One major risk that investors should keep a close eye on is Nucor's significant exposure to the price of scrap metal due to the company's reliance on electric arc furnaces (EAF). The circular steelmaking process is often preferred due to quality of the steel and the whole process being significantly less emissions-intensive. However, this also puts NUE's cost structure at risk as supply of scrap metal is inelastic.

To partially solve this problem, Nucor produces a significant amount of scrap metal through The David J. Joseph Company and its affiliates ("DJJ").

DJJ operates six regional scrap recycling companies across the United States that together have shredders capable of processing approximately 5,648,000 tons of ferrous scrap annually

Source: Nucor 2024 Annual Report.

Some of its peers, such as Cleveland-Cliffs, have also been moving into the scrap business, as future prices are expected to increase as more EAF capacity comes online.

As a matter of fact, the CEO of Cliffs sees this as a major risk for EAF operators and anticipates tightening of the scrap market.

The first step is the tightening of the scrap market. We have been saying for years that the continued push toward EAFs would force the scrap price higher. That's exactly what's happening now. Prime scrap supply is inelastic and demand keeps growing. In just two months, we have seen prime prices move up $70 per gross ton. Meanwhile, Cleveland-Cliffs is sitting exactly where we need to be. Our ROI-based operations give us cost stability, quality consistency and supply security. This is a long-term advantage that we will only get stronger over time.

Source: Cleveland-Cliffs Q4 2024 Earnings Transcript (emphasis added).

As I said earlier, this is a major risk for long-term investors in NUE, although it should be mentioned that this is a risk that has been on Cliffs CEO's mind for quite some time (emphasis added):

The timing of the start-up of HBI plant is extraordinarily positive for us with the obvious scarcity of domestic prime scrap in the marketplace. This scarcity of scrap should only grow as new EAFs start up in the United States, and we will have more bias for the same scrap. Good for our HBI and good for Cleveland-Cliffs.

Source: Cleveland-Cliffs Q4 2020 Earnings Transcript.

So, scrap is not an easy thing to go by, so, scrap prices should go up as well. So, we are entering unchartered territories, interesting times, but it's something that we here at Cleveland-Cliffs are absolutely prepared for.

Source: Cleveland-Cliffs Q4 2018 Earnings Transcript.

Therefore, personally, I wouldn't put too much emphasis on this risk in the near-term, but it is definitely something that long-term investors of NUE should factor in.

Conclusion

Investors are increasingly interested in the U.S. Steel sector, and with that, high-quality businesses, such as Nucor, are now in the spotlight. In spite of certain risks, NUE is among the most attractive stocks in the space and is appealing to more long-term oriented investors. While some peers could outperform in the short term due to some catalysts, Nucor stock remains a solid long-term investment.

data.title: Will Brent Oil Recover? Trade Tensions And Inventory Data To Watch
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Brent crude prices were up around 1% in the Asian session but have since turned red for the day following the European open.

Oil prices recovered from their lowest point in nearly four years during the Asian session as worries persist that U.S. tariffs could hurt demand and trigger a global recession. Caution remains as there are still risks of further declines in the days ahead.

As of Monday, Brent and WTI had slumped 14% and 15% respectively following U.S. President Donald Trump's April 2 announcement of "reciprocal tariffs" on all imports.

The situation remains risky as President Trump threatened to add a 50% tariff on Chinese goods if China doesn’t remove its 34% retaliatory tariff yesterday. It’s unlikely China will change its stance with comments overnight hinting as much, which means tensions could rise further, adding to concerns about slower growth and weaker oil demand.

OPEC + concerns

Crude oil prices dropping since April 2 suggest the market sees a higher chance of a recession. The sharp decline will likely concern OPEC+, which surprised everyone last week by announcing a bigger supply increase for May.

If prices keep falling, OPEC+ might stop or even reverse these increases. Saudi Arabia needs around $90 per barrel to balance its budget according to ING. While last week’s supply hike shows they’re not targeting this price, they likely don’t want to see oil prices drop much further below their breakeven point.

A Reuters report yesterday revealed OPEC's oil production dropped in March before a planned output increase, according to a Reuters survey. Nigeria reduced supplies to local refineries, and U.S. efforts to cut exports from Iran and Venezuela had an impact. OPEC produced 26.63 million barrels per day last month, 110,000 barrels per day less than in February, with the biggest declines from Nigeria, Iran, and Venezuela.

Is there a silver lining for Oil prices?

Market participants looking at the supply and demand picture may have a small reason to be optimistic. As with OPEC +, if Oil prices drop too far the profitability comes into question which could help lessen supply and provide some support to prices.

For example, a slowdown in U.S. drilling could give the market some support. There is a case to be made that with current WTI prices a drop in drilling activity becomes a real possibility. This could eventually lead to slower supply growth or even a drop in U.S. oil production. U.S. shale production declines quickly, so constant drilling is needed to maintain stable output. According to the Dallas Federal Reserve Energy survey, producers need about $65 per barrel to profitably drill a new well.

The longer WTI prices hover below the $65 a barrel, the greater the probability that drilling activity may start to be impacted. This is definitely worth monitoring.

Inventories data due later this week

A preliminary Reuters poll on Monday suggested that U.S. crude oil and distillate stockpiles likely increased by about 1.6 million barrels last week, pointing to weak demand. The American Petroleum Institute is set to release inventory data on Tuesday, with official numbers from the Energy Information Administration coming on Wednesday.

Technical Analysis - Brent Crude

This is a follow-up analysis of my prior report “Brent Oil price plummets: OPEC+ output hike and price outlook” published on April 1, 2025.

From a technical analysis standpoint, Brent has printed an indecision doji candle on the daily timeframe, which could hint at a potential recovery.

The period 14-RSI is in overbought territory and may be used as a confirmation of sorts that momentum has shifted. When the RSI crosses back above the 30 level, this could be a sign.

Immediate resistance rests at 66.42 and 68.17 before the 69.52 and 70.00 psychological handle come into focus.

Having broken above the descending trendline Brent looked poised for further gain earlier this week. However, as discussed in the article on April 1, the fundamentals did not support the technicals completely. Downside risk was always present, with growth concerns always likely to arise from the tariff announcements.

A move lower from current price and yesterday's lows are the first area of support resting at 62.76 before the 61.00 and psychological 60.oo handle comes into focus.

For now though, Oil will be driven by overall market sentiment as well as developments around the tariff picture moving forward.

Brent Crude Oil Daily Chart, April 8, 2025

Support

  • 62.76
  • 61.00
  • 60.00 (psychological level)

Resistance

  • 66.42
  • 68.17
  • 69.52

Original Post

data.title: Alignment Healthcare: Not Aligned For My Portfolio, Despite Great News
data.description:
One interesting company that saw its share price rise by roughly 7% on April 8th, even after markets turned lower again as a result of new damaging trade policies in the US, was Alignment Healthcare (NASDAQ:ALHC). This came after news broke that Medicare Advantage plans have seen approval for a 5.06% increase in payments from the US government from 2025 to 2026. As a player in the health care market that is heavily involved in the Medicare Advantage ecosystem and that caters specifically to seniors, Alignment Healthcare stands to benefit from this development. We won't know the full impact of it, but we do know that this enterprise has already been growing rapidly in recent years. Management has also forecasted continued growth for 2025.

For those looking for a growth-oriented prospect, especially one that focuses on the healthcare space, Alignment Healthcare is certainly worth considering. Having said that, as a value investor, not even the rapid growth that the business has achieved and will achieve moving forward justifies a bullish assessment in my book. At this time, I would argue that, at best, Alignment Healthcare deserves a ‘hold’ rating because of how early stage it is in terms of getting to cash flow positive results.

A niche healthcare play

If you're not familiar with Alignment Healthcare, it's worth noting that management describes the company as a consumer-centric platform that is focused on improving health care experiences for seniors. In essence, the firm offers up its own Medicare Advantage plans that it customizes for the seniors that utilize its platform. Using its own predictive algorithms that are fed by medical and demographic data that the business collects; it helps to identify the risks of a significant medical event that could come about on a customer-by-customer basis. Using its employed clinical teams that it calls Care Anywhere; the firm helps to provide care for those with chronic conditions and to provide other aid in order to reduce hospital admissions and readmissions in the long run.

The goal here is to ultimately lower health care costs. For each of its customers, it receives premiums from the federal government based on its contract with the CMS (Centers for Medicine & Medicaid Services). In exchange for this consistent cash flow, the enterprise promises to cover all of the healthcare costs for its customers, irrespective of how high those costs might be. There are certain adjustments made to this based on risk factors that come from the medical diagnosis of its members. But at the end of the day, it's best to view the company as a way for the government to eliminate its risk associated with health outcomes of senior citizens. This is known as capitation and has become quite popular in the health care industry.

In 2024, the firm also entered into a management services and risk management agreement with a third-party health care provider that has assumed responsibility for arranging and controlling health care services provided to its customers that are under its ACO REACH business. But this accounts for only a small portion of its customer base at this time.

Over the past few years, Alignment Healthcare has seen some rather rapid growth. From 2022 to 2024, revenue for the company nearly doubled, from $1.43 billion to $2.70 billion. This rise in sales was driven largely by a surge in the number of health plan memberships from 98,400 to 189,100. Management defines these memberships as the number of members enrolled in its HMO and PPO contracts as of the end of each fiscal year. This doesn't factor in any members that the company has that include third-party payers, and it also excludes its ACO REACH members. Third party payer members totaled only 400 in each of the last two fiscal years. And in 2024, the number of ACO REACH members amounted to 8,300.

Even though revenue has risen, the company still generates significant net losses. Last year, it generated a net loss of $128 million. Fortunately, this did mark an improvement over the $149.5 million that the firm lost in 2022. Other profitability metrics have also improved. Take operating cash flow as an example. Over the last three years, it has gone from negative $45.4 million to positive $34.8 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, it has improved only slightly from negative $39.9 million to negative $26.1 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company has improved from negative $26.7 million to positive $1.3 million.

A key operating metric for the company is referred to as MBR (Medical Benefits Ratio). This is calculated by dividing the total medical expenses of the company incurs on behalf of its customers, excluding any depreciation, equity compensation, and clinical restructuring costs, by the revenue generated over the same window of time. By law, the business has to pay out at least 85% of its premiums toward covering medical expenses. That leaves the remaining 15% that can go toward administrative costs and profits. The goal is to ensure that companies in this space are providing adequate care for their policyholders. Over the last three years, this number has actually increased from 86.5% to 88.8%. This means that it is spending more of its premiums on customers now as a percent of what it charges than it did previously. This means that any improvement on the bottom line has really just been the result of operating efficiencies and its success in scaling its revenue.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year, management expects revenue to surge to between $3.72 billion and $3.775 billion. At the midpoint, this would represent an increase of 38.6% compared to what the company generated last year. They expect this to be driven by a rise in the number of members on its platform to between 227,000 and 233,000. The company is certainly on its way, as evidenced by the fact that, as of January 1st, of this year, it had 209,900 members. On the bottom line, management anticipates EBITDA of between $35 million and $60 million. At the midpoint, the $47.5 million generated by the company would imply an EBITDA margin of 1.3%.

To understand if there might be any value here at the moment, I need only direct you to the chart below. In it, you can see how much adjusted operating cash flow Alignment Healthcare would need to generate in order to trade at either 5 times, 10 times, or 15 times, on a price to adjusted operating cash flow basis. You can also see how much EBITDA the company would need to generate in order to trade at the same multiples on an EV to EBITDA basis.

In the chart below, you can also see the margins that these cash flow metrics would need to generate in order for these cash flow figures to become a reality. At the high end of the multiple range, we are still looking at significant increases over what management expects when it comes to the EBITDA margin, for instance, for the 2025 fiscal year. This indicates to me that while the company doesn't have an impossible path that it needs to eventually follow, it does look expensive even factoring in current growth.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I do think that Alignment Healthcare is an interesting business. However, it's not the kind of prospect for me. The announcement that payments for Medicare Advantage will increase will certainly help the company from a revenue perspective. But at the end of the day, the firm still has ways to go if it is ever to be attractively priced. I wouldn't say that the picture is so disheartening that the company deserves a bearish assessment. But at this time, the best that I can have it rated is a ‘hold’ for now.

data.title: Halliburton: Pullback Is Indicative Of An Expected Recession
data.description:
Halliburton Company's (NYSE:HAL) stock has come under significant pressure in recent days due to a double hit from tariff announcements and increased OPEC production. The company's share price is now approaching levels that I consider indicative of a recession, providing a potentially attractive entry point, particularly if the tariff situation deescalates before a recession occurs.

I previously felt that 2025 was likely to be challenging for Halliburton and suggested that $20 per share was a more attractive entry point. This was largely due to a belief that weak demand growth and solid supply growth would weigh on upstream activity levels.

While there is still substantial downside risk if a deep recession occurs, it is hard not to feel that Halliburton is now reasonably priced. Drilling and completion activity levels probably need to begin to trough before Halliburton's share price rebounds. In addition, investors should keep in mind that in the current situation there may be little policy support for asset prices, at least in the short term.

Market Conditions

While tariffs have long been expected, what was announced was far more aggressive than generally anticipated, leading to a sharp correction in equities. Effective tariffs in the 25-30% range are expected, which would be the highest level in over a century. Tariffs are also generally expected to begin moving lower later this year, which has probably prevented equities from falling further. This is a dynamic situation, though, which is dependent on both the willingness of trade partners to negotiate and whether tariffs are viewed as a negotiating tool or a necessary long-term barrier to support the reshoring of manufacturing.

Tariffs are also occurring against a fairly weak macro backdrop. The housing market remains a risk and the current administration is likely to provide the economy with far less fiscal stimulus. The potential inflationary impulse created by tariffs will also likely prevent the Fed from pursuing looser monetary policy, absent a recession.

The severity of this situation for Halliburton has been compounded by an unexpectedly large increase in OPEC production. OPEC+ recently agreed to raise crude oil production by 411,000 bpd, 3x the amount anticipated. Ensuring compliance is a major issue though and the move may have been done in part to discourage countries like Kazakhstan, Iraq, and Russia from overproducing. Despite the planned increase, OPEC production remains well off recent highs, which is an ongoing threat to the oil market.

The combination of tariffs and increased OPEC production has driven oil prices to pre-COVID levels and there could be further downside if economic conditions weaken. Goldman Sachs is now forecasting a $62 per bbl WTI crude oil price (CL1:COM) in December 2025, citing tariffs and higher OPEC+ production. S&P Global Market Intelligence also believes that oil demand growth could be reduced by 500,000 bpd in 2025. Natural gas prices are a bright spot, although I continue to believe that this is meaningless to Halliburton due to the relatively small amount of onshore natural gas activity in the U.S.

The rig count in the U.S. remains fairly stable at the moment, although the frac spread count continues to decline, which is arguably the more important metric for Halliburton. While upstream activity in the U.S. hasn't been overly impacted so far, oil prices are approaching levels where CapEx is likely to drop. For example, a recent Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas survey puts breakeven oil prices in the 61-70 USD per bbl range, dependent on the basin.

The number of Halliburton job openings doesn't point towards a collapse in activity yet, although this is probably just due to the fact that the current weakness hasn't worked its way through the market yet. If rig and frac spread counts are correct in the coming weeks, Halliburton is likely to move aggressively to cut its cost base.

While Halliburton's financials are still fairly strong at the moment, there has been some deterioration in performance in recent quarters. Unless there is a sharp shift in monetary and fiscal policy, the company's revenue and margins are likely to come under further pressure in the coming quarters. This has been problematic for Halliburton's share price as the company's 2025 guidance implied a reacceleration in the second half of the year, which is now highly unlikely to be realized.

Halliburton's exposure to unconventionals is also a weakness at the moment, although could lead to outperformance when the market rebounds. As a result, it has not been a surprise to see Halliburton's stock underperform peers in recent months. ProFrac Holding Corp.'s (ACDC) share price also also fallen significantly, due both to its exposure to the onshore pressure pumping market in the U.S. and its weak balance sheet.

While Halliburton's stock has appeared reasonably priced for the past 2 years based on earnings, this was a mirage caused by unsustainably high margins. Halliburton's revenue multiple is now approaching trough levels, although investors probably should be prepared for a 15-30% revenue drop in the event of a recession. If a meaningful downturn does occur, Halliburton's earnings are likely to fall significantly, resulting in the company's P/E ratio becoming meaningless.

While $20 per share is likely a reasonable entry point, investors should be prepared for further downside if onshore U.S. activity levels begin to drop rapidly. A better entry point could be when activity levels begin to stabilize, even if this is at a depressed level. A quick end to tariffs could see Halliburton's share price rebound sharply, although OPEC production would likely cap the size of the move. In addition, retaliatory tariffs remain a risk, as is the possibility of tariffs remaining in place for the long term.

data.title: U.S. Recession Odds Just Hit 62%: Trade Carefully
data.description:
Wild trading in stocks continued this week, with the S&P 500 (SPY) swinging 7% from its low on Monday. As of my writing this on Tuesday, the market has given the vast majority of that gain back. The proximate cause for the sell-off is a radical shift in the amount of tariffs being collected in the US and abroad. However, the root causes run much deeper, raising questions about peak valuations near the dot-com peak, years of wild speculation in the markets, and the after-effects of a 15-year period where interest rates were close to zero. I could tell you what I think about all of this, but if you're a long-time reader of mine, then you've heard most of it before. Instead, I'll give you some data points that will help make decisions about buying and selling easier. Also, I'll avoid posting price graphs as by the time you read this, they could easily be stale.

#1 (Really) Understand Volatility

Volatility measures how much the prices of assets fluctuate over time. Changes in price are chaotic but not totally random. In fact, professional traders and market makers make a substantial amount of their income off of understanding volatility and creating systems to take advantage of it. The first step to understanding volatility is measuring it. Portfolio managers generally measure volatility by standard deviation. This is an important point, but calculating it is beyond the scope of this article. Instead, I'll give you a shorthand to estimate volatility.

There's an index called the volatility index, or VIX (VIX) for short. If you watch CNBC, it's constantly one of the indexes that they quote. The VIX measures the approximate annualized volatility in the market over the next 30 days. For this to be useful, you need to do some shorthand math. For the expected change over the next month, you divide the VIX by the square root of 12 (months in a year), and for the expected change over the next day, you divide by the square root of 251 (business days in a year). I'm writing this on Tuesday afternoon, and the VIX is about 53. This means that the market is expected to move approximately 15.3% over the next month in either direction and about 3.3% on each trading day. This is a very high level of volatility, as the average VIX is around 17-18, implying that the stock market moves around 1% per day in the long run. In practice, there are also times when volatility is low, and in those times the market might only move 0.5% per day or so.

If you're a retail investor, and you're reading this, the most important thing to know is that large market swings for random reasons or rumors are normal right now and will be for the foreseeable future. Sometimes you'll buy, and you'll think you're getting a great price, only to see prices tank even more. And if you're running riskier positions or trading with leverage, you might find that you need to cut risk at or near the worst possible times. In that case, risk management is a cost of doing business, and you'll give back a portion of the gains that you make in bull markets to properly manage risk. If you fail to do so, you run the risk of busting your account.

#2 Understand Signal Vs. Noise

The classic strategy for making money in volatile markets is to assume everything is noise and to buy assets you like on big selloffs. The old-school theory here says that volatility is noise, and if you can pick a reasonably stable business with a volatile share price, then you're winning anytime you can get it cheaply. This is a reasonable strategy, and it works, but it has limits. Sometimes a falling share price is more than noise, it's actually a signal that something is wrong with the business. For example, hordes of retail traders on Reddit bought stock in Silicon Valley Bank and First Republic Bank in 2023 shortly before they went bust. For a bank, the share price is not noise because it reflects the stability of its deposits and ability to sell shares to raise capital. That doesn't mean that you can never buy banks cheaply in a crisis, but it does mean that you're running a far bigger risk than if you find stocks like Philip Morris International Inc. (PM) or The Coca-Cola Company (KO) trading for 20-30% off.

Similarly, the market is going to swing faster than you're going to be able to know why as a retail trader. Wall Street trading desks have better access to information than you and can trade faster than you are. For example, if a trade deal happens, the market will likely know before you or I will, and there will be no way to tell if it's a false rumor or true (signal or noise). So don't try to compete with professional trading firms over short time periods – you'll lose. However, if you take a deep breath and look at what the market is offering you at any given time, you're likely to see assets trading below fair value.

A small example of this is the big sell-off in municipal bonds earlier this week. Investment-grade munis, iShares National Muni Bond ETF (MUB), are down about 3% this week. Does this reflect a huge jump in credit risk? Nope. It's because hedge funds that own munis are getting margin calls on their other bad trades, and they have to sell them. Their loss can be your gain. Taking advantage of market dislocations in high-quality bonds is easy to do. Could you make more money by buying the right stocks? Sure, but that's the beauty of having cash in a down market: You get to choose where you want to deploy your capital, and you'll have a lot of good options.

#3: Leverage: Be Behind The Gun, Not Under It

One of the reasons for market volatility is that leveraged players are forced sellers. There are a lot more people than you think who use leverage to make stock market bets, and retail traders who bet on the market using options are largely serviced by professional market-making firms who have to buy and sell stocks to balance their books behind the scenes. The current bull market has seen an explosive rise in the number of people using leveraged trades of various kinds, which set the conditions for these types of selloffs to happen today.

Over the last 10 years, options trading volume is up 4x, allowing retail investors to easily make leveraged bets on the market with their downside limited to their investment. Over the past 20 years, volume is up closer to 10x.

Options trading exploded around COVID, the same time market valuations on popular stocks and the indexes came a little unhinged. I believe that this is no accident. This pushes a lot of leverage into the market that market makers have to deal with.

This means a few things–

  1. If you're thinking of making options bets right now, you should largely forget it. The market makers don't want your risk and will charge you crazy spreads to trade. Shares are volatile enough right now, so trade those instead until the VIX is below 25. The flip side of this is that if the market continues to go haywire, you may often have a profitable but risky trade in selling index puts at 60% annualized volatility or more. Of course, if the market does crash, you end up owing a huge amount. I dislike these kinds of trades at the start of a big market selloff, but you could consider them at a much lower valuation.
  2. Leveraged ETFs will crush you for similar reasons. Because the funds are forced to reset to their target leverage at the end of each day, they become predictable forced sellers on big down days, much to the delight of Wall Street trading desks. This is called volatility drag, and it's a massive money loser for unwitting traders. On the flip side, it can create profitable short-term trading opportunities because you know who is selling late in the day and roughly how much they're forced to sell by the close.
  3. Leveraged trading has allowed valuations to rise above historically normal levels. While I can't prove a direct link between people making leveraged bets and valuations, I can point out that current valuations are higher than average even after the correction. Current valuations are roughly the same now as they were right before COVID for the S&P 500, and to get down to where the market traded on Christmas Eve of 2018 when the last non-COVID market panic was, we'd need to get to around 13 times earnings. That means that if we bottom at the same valuation we did in 2018, stocks would need to go down another 25% or so. That would take us to around 3750 on the S&P, which is far cheaper than the current price. If there's indeed a recession and earnings estimates come down, one could easily take off another 20% or so from this number.

You're getting a deal if you buy stocks now, but not a historically crazy one.

Will There Be A Recession?

The offshore betting market thinks so. Polymarket is currently pricing a 62% chance of a U.S. recession this year, which is up from 56% when I wrote my last article. Interesting things are going on in various corners of the world, from a looming Florida real estate crash to Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) pulling back on AI orders and OPEC flooding the market to drive down oil prices. To conclude, I went back to the same sushi restaurant from last week, and this time there was no CNBC on TV – I had the bar to myself. Instead of the financial advisor arguing with his client, this time it was silent.

data.title: Voya Small Company Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio review

U.S. stocks continued their upward trajectory during the fourth quarter following Donald Trump's presidential victory, with the S&P 500 Index rising by 2.41% and the Nasdaq Composite advancing by 6.17%. The consumer discretionary and communication services sectors led, while materials and healthcare lagged. Large cap stocks outperformed small caps, and growth stocks significantly beat value stocks.

The U.S. bond market struggled during the quarter on concerns about sticky inflation and the U.S. Federal Reserve's more conservative rate-cut path. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index declined by 3.06% and the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rose by more than 80 basis points (bp), ending the quarter at 4.58%. The Fed cut rates by 25 bp in November and December. However, the central bank now projects just two rate cuts in 2025, reflecting a more cautious stance in response to strong economic data.

The Fund outperformed the Index on a NAV basis for the quarter ending December 31, 2024, primarily due to a positive impact from both stock selection and the active allocation bets. The Fund benefited from the overweight and selection in the industrials sector and the underweight and selection in the health care sector. Conversely, selection in the financials and consumer discretionary sectors detracted.

The main individual contributors to performance were GEO Group Inc., CoreCivic, Inc. and Excelerate Energy, Inc. The overweight positions in The GEO Group, Inc. (GEO), CoreCivic, Inc. (CXW) and Excelerate Energy, Inc. (EE) were driven by the machine learning model's favorable assessments, with each stock ranking in the top 20% of its peer group at the start of the quarter.

For GEO, the model's positive view was primarily influenced by its strong valuation, size, and leverage metrics. In the valuation category, earnings before interest and taxes (EBITDA) and cash flow from operations (CFO)- to-enterprise value (EV) ratios were particularly compelling relative to peers. The stock experienced a significant rise post-election as the incoming administration's proposed stricter immigration enforcement policies were expected to benefit GEO, which manages immigration centers.

CXW also earned a favorable model score due to its robust valuation metrics, notably book-to-price and EBITDA ratios. CXW, which owns and operates prisons and residential reentry centers, saw its stock price increase after reporting strong 3Q24 2024 earnings and providing a positive outlook for 2025, bolstered by anticipated policies from the Republican administration.

EE's overweight position stemmed from the model's high conviction in its valuation, quality, and leverage features. Within valuation, the stock stood out for its book-to-price and EBITDA metrics. EE's stock rose following a strong 3Q24 earnings report, favorable industry tailwinds and growth potential stemming from its under-leveraged balance sheet.

The primary detractors from performance were Option Care Health Inc., IonQ, Inc. and Avanos Medical, Inc. Option Care Health, Inc. (OPCH), a provider of home care services to clients with private health insurance, declined during the quarter following management's decision to lower future revenue guidance.

The machine learning (ML) model's conviction in the stock was driven by its strong quality features (capital acquisition ratio, buyback yield and return on invested capital (ROIC), combined with attractive size and volatility metrics. Despite the decline, the models maintained their positive outlook at year-end. Not owning IonQ, Inc. (IONQ), a technology stock specializing in quantum computing, detracted from relative returns during the quarter.

The ML model held a negative view of the stock based on its size, quality (ROIC, governance, operating margin) and valuation (book-to-price, EBITDA) features. However, the stock rose after reporting stronger-than-expected third-quarter earnings and announcing a well-received acquisition that bolstered its position as a quantum computing leader. The models maintained their negative view at year-end, choosing to remain on the sidelines.

The overweight position in Avanos Medical, Inc. (AVNS), a healthcare stock, was driven by the model's favorable assessment of its valuation features (book-to-price and EBITDA). However, the stock struggled during the quarter due to lackluster third-quarter results, reduced guidance and the announcement of its CEO's retirement. Despite these challenges, the models retained their conviction in the stock at year-end.

Current strategy and outlook

After months of noise surrounding the U.S. presidential election, markets have now refocused on macroeconomic data, which offer mixed signals. Key concerns include global geopolitical tensions, especially around tariffs, and deteriorating sentiment tied to megacap positioning and broader market weakness.

Despite these challenges, U.S. equities should continue to benefit from robust consumer spending, optimism around artificial intelligence and solid corporate earnings. U.S. inflation is projected at 2.5% for 2025, but the new administration's policies could reverse the disinflation trend.

Holdings detail

Companies mentioned in this report percentage of Fund investments, as of 12/31/24: GEO Group Inc. 1.01%, CoreCivic, Inc. 0.90%, Excelerate Energy, Inc. 0.98%, Option Care Health, Inc. 0.62%, IonQ, Inc. 0.00% and Avanos Medical, Inc.0.52%, 0.00% indicates that the security is no longer in the portfolio. Portfolio holdings are subject to change on a daily basis.

The Russell 2000 Index is an unmanaged index that measures the performance of securities of smaller U.S. companies. It is a small-cap stock market index that makes up the smallest 2,000 stocks in the Russell 3000 Index.Index returns do not reflect fees, brokerage commissions, taxes or other expenses of investing. Investors cannot invest directly in an index.

All investing involves risks of fluctuating prices and the uncertainties of rates of return and yield inherent in investing. You could lose money on your investment and any of the following risks, among others, could affect investment performance. The following principal risks are presented in alphabetical order which does not imply order of importance or likelihood: Company; Currency; Derivative Instruments; Environmental, Social, and Governance (Equity); Foreign (Non-U.S.) Investments; Growth Investing; Investment Model; Liquidity; Market; Market Disruption and Geopolitical; Other Investment Companies; Portfolio Turnover; Real Estate Companies and Real Estate Investment Trusts; Securities Lending; Small-Capitalization Company; Value Investing.

Investors should consult the Fund's Prospectus and Statement of Additional Information for a more detailed discussion of the Fund's risks.

Artificial intelligence (AI) including natural language processing, machine learning, and other forms of AI may pose inherent risks, including but not limited to: issues with data privacy, intellectual property, consumer protection, and anti-discrimination laws; ethics and transparency concerns; information security issues; the potential for unfair bias and discrimination; quality and accuracy of inputs and outputs; technical failures and potential misuse. Reliance on information produced using AI-based technology and tools should factor in these risks.

The Voya Machine Intelligence (VMI) team employs a proprietary machine learning approach to identify and exploit persistent patterns in company data. The approach leverages (non-linear) machine learning ("ML") models for fundamental analysis. The ML models employed do not utilize generative AI algorithms.

The strategy employs a quantitative model to execute the strategy. Data imprecision, software or other technology malfunctions, programming inaccuracies and similar circumstances may impair the performance of these systems, which may negatively affect performance. Furthermore, there can be no assurance that the quantitative models used in managing the strategy will perform as anticipated or enable the strategy to achieve its objective.

The strategy is available as a mutual fund or variable portfolio. The mutual fund may be available to you as part of your employer sponsored retirement plan. There may be additional plan level fees resulting in personal performance that varies from stated performance. Please call your benefits office for more information.Variable annuities and group annuities are long-term investments designed for retirement purposes. If withdrawals are taken prior to age 59½, an IRS 10% premature distribution penalty tax may apply. Money taken from the annuity will be taxed as ordinary income in the year the money is distributed. An annuity does not provide any additional tax deferral benefit, as tax deferral is provided by the plan. Annuities may be subject to additional fees and expenses to which other tax-qualified funding vehicles may not be subject. However, an annuity does provide other features and benefits, such as lifetime income payments and death benefits, which may be valuable to you. All guarantees are based on the financial strength and claims paying ability of the issuing insurance company, who is solely responsible for all obligations under its policies. data.title: The Economic Reality Of Tariffs
data.description:
Nothing has been more important to the business and investment community than the implementation of widespread tariffs on virtually all trading partners.

The arguments or the stated goals of tariff policy are clear – protecting current American jobs from unfair foreign competition, reshoring manufacturing plants and high-paying jobs, and closing large trade deficits.

However, the economic reality of tariffs is unlikely to achieve these goals for the following reasons.

1) Tariffs Are A Tax

Tariffs raise the price of imported goods. Tariffs are not always passed through to the end consumer. Sometimes, businesses will absorb the increase in price in the form of a lower profit margin, but after-tax national income suffers.

2) Tariffs Protect Inefficient Industries

The reason businesses move out of a country is because another country has a competitive advantage in production. This dynamic increases the efficiency of capital. Tariffs blunt this advantage and encourage less efficient uses of capital. A business no longer has to compete on quality or price. This encourages stagnation over innovation.

3) Tariffs Drive a Misallocation of Resources

One of the most common knocks on excessive government intervention in the economy is that it picks winners and losers rather than letting the free market purely dictate the allocation of capital. Tariffs are not different, as they place a government-directed hand on the allocation of capital.

4) Tariffs Increase Unemployment In The Final Analysis

In the first level analysis, tariffs can protect jobs in the targeted industry, but the second and third level analysis often show an overall increase in unemployment. There is an uncomfortable political trade-off. Free trade may negatively impact some domestic industries that get competed away in exchange for cheaper goods for the broader public. This negatively impacts one sector of the economy for a generalized boost in consumption for the larger segment of the economy. If goods become cheaper, consumers have more after-tax disposable income to spend on other goods and services, creating jobs in those sectors. Tariffs work in the opposite direction, helping a smaller domestic industry in exchange for more expensive goods for the broader public, reducing after-tax disposable income to spend on other goods and services, hurting growth and employment in those sectors. If the goal is to maximize overall GDP per capita, a proxy for the overall standard of living, tariffs strongly work against this goal.

5) The Fallacy of Trade Deficits

At face value, it seems that a trade deficit is negative for the deficit country, but trade balances naturally adjust through currency and capital flows. Closing the trade deficit is not necessarily a desirable goal. A trade deficit implies foreign capital is a source of savings in the National Savings and Investment Identity. How we utilize these savings is a different question.

6) Tariffs Often Lead to Retaliation

Tariffs and trade wars often lead to a generalized increase in the world tariff level, which furthers all the negative points listed above. In most cases, if a country places a tariff on you, that country is experiencing negative economic costs in doing so, as outlined in the prior five points. Retaliating with your own tariffs imposes those same negative consequences on you.

National Security Exception

In some cases, it is not wise to have critical resources dependent on a hostile nation. In this case, maintaining some domestic production for the sake of national security is likely appropriate. However, the reason for the tariff policy doesn’t negate the economic consequences. You simply trade improved national security for negative economic consequences. In some cases, this could be a strategic move.

The Bottom Line

Increasing the overall global or domestic tariff level is not a path to an improved standard of living. Tariffs have an undeniable economic cost that moves a country away from maximizing the growth rate in the standard of living. Trade policy is a complex political matter because there are winners and losers to the free trade option and the protectionist option. However, it is clear that the free trade option has the most positive net effect on the overall standard of living.

To be clear, if the end result of these tariff wars is a decrease in the global tariff level, that would be a long-run benefit to the United States and the global economy, less any short-term disruptions in achieving that result. If the end result is an increase in the global tariff level, negative economic consequences will be felt by the United States and the global economy.

data.title: Diamondback Energy: Fast-Growing Dividend Now Has A Tempting Yield
data.description:
The common stock of Diamondback Energy, Inc. (NASDAQ:FANG) is currently being treated like all the other common stocks as Mr. Market is in the “throw them all out” mood. But eventually things will settle down and quality will matter. Diamondback Energy is usually known for fast growth. The debt rating is investment grade as well. That fast growth includes the dividend, which is now on the high end of the historical yield.

Normally, one does not see a yield like the above for a company that grows as fast as Diamondback Energy. This management “goes the extra mile” by having a very long history of acquisitions that have grown the company (and the stock price) while meeting the market demand for shareholder returns.

Dividends (the variable part) can vary because this company will switch between stock repurchases and a variable payment as market conditions dictate. However, as the yield shows above, things are getting into irrationally cheap territory with a dividend yield that one would expect from a slower growth company.

The latest base dividend is shown above. Growth in per share metrics not only happens from that acquisition program that has proven successful, but also from the share repurchase program that generally adds another percent or so to that growth. The complete program has enabled a growth of both the base dividend and the variable dividend at rates far above the industry average.

Now, as the company gets large, the dividend growth rate of really any kind will slow. However, as long as management continues the acquisition history, this idea could prove to be a solid variable income idea for a retirement portfolio. The company is investment grade, and the stock price rarely gets so low that the overall cash yield is that high.

Last Article

The last article noted that there was yet another acquisition to add to a long line of successful acquisitions. It also mentioned that the CEO would be stepping down. This may usher in a year or two of uncertainty. But it would appear that there has been some succession planning. That should smooth the transition to the new CEO.

Management did announce that the acquisition mentioned in the last article closed on April 1.

The exceptionally low costs are shown by the breakeven price needed to defend the base dividend.

The company has expanded into the Midland Basin because acreage prices are much lower than the Delaware Basin and profitability appears to be every bit as good. Acquisitions can be made at prices that benefit per share metrics. Then, if Mr. Market decides to ignore the stock, then management will buy back shares to make the deal even better. The investment grade rating is maintained by selling any acreage that no longer can compete for capital.

Fourth Quarter

The net result of all of this is that per share metrics grow significantly, as shown below year-in and longer term.

Like any other upstream company, Diamondback is exposed to the commodity price fluctuations. But if management is correct about the benefits of the latest acquisition, the stock price is very likely to recover and move to higher ground during the next cyclical recovery.

The investment grade rating means that this company is in a position to deal with whatever happens in the future. The debt rating here is higher than is the case for many companies I follow. The company has an exceptionally low breakeven point to pay the base dividend as well. This is a very unusual picture in total when one considers this is an upstream company.

With many companies, I follow that base dividend often proves to be indefensible during cyclical downturns because the WTI price needed to pay it is too high. Here, things are considerably better. More importantly, if the dividend needs to be cut, investors can be sure that investment grade rating means it will be back sooner than many other companies I follow.

This management already shows a flexibility that one seldom sees. The acquisition program has slowed the growth of costs in the long term. If debt needs to come down, then assets are sold to repay that debt. There is a firm believe that (if it comes to it) the first loss is the best loss, and then management moves on. That puts this management far ahead of others that often management to magnify a problem or challenge into a fatal situation.

Summary

The overall economic situation has changed enough that the original fiscal year 2025 guidance may well be modified as the fiscal year continues.

This company has low enough costs that it should not be affected as much by the current turmoil as is the case with many other upstream competitors. However, this company can also acquire another company to make up for any production loss should it decide to allow production to decline due to low commodity prices. That gives this management unusual flexibility during a time of weak commodity prices.

There is also the choice to grow the company production at commodity prices that are not profitable for many other companies.

Overall, the flexibility demonstrated by the management strategy reinforces the strong buy idea. This is a company that can be owned during a time of uncertainty like the present.

Good management tends to outperform when it is least expected. While others are waiting for “the storm to pass” (and this is quite a storm), this management is likely figuring out how to improve results that will show during the inevitable recovery.

The oil and gas industry tends to outperform during recoveries. This company will likely be a stock price leader during any recovery. Even if the investor tends to hold through the current period of uncertainty, this is a stock that should treat an investor well during the long run.

Risks

The article is written with the idea that the current situation with tariffs and trade wars is a bargaining type of situation that will be replaced with something that is constructive and permanent. There is no assurance that will happen.

Any upstream company is subject to the low visibility and volatility of future commodity prices. A severe and sustained downturn will materially change the outlook of the company.

This company has unusually low costs and an investment grade rating on its debt. It should perform better than many upstream operators during a cyclical downturn. However, there are no guarantees on that idea.

A loss of key personnel could prove to be a major setback for the future of the company.

data.title: Amcor: Dividend Aristocrat Executing A Major Acquisition
data.description:
Shares of packaging concern Amcor plc (NYSE:AMCR) have been range-bound since the November 2024 announcement of its intention to acquire Berry Global (BERY) in an all-stock transaction. Due to close in mid-2025, the combination is expected to wring out $650 million in annual synergies by year 3 plus a one-time $280 million cash benefit from working capital efficiencies. With membership in the S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats (current yield: 5.7%), the positive reaction from Street analysts concerning the Berry deal and recent insider buying merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview

Amcor plc is a Jersey domiciled, Zurich, Switzerland headquartered global manufacturer of packaging solutions primarily to the food, beverage, and healthcare verticals. The company’s operations currently include 212 facilities in 40 countries, employing more than 41,000. Those numbers will rise considerably upon the closing of an impending merger with rival Berry Global Group (BERY). Amcor was founded as a paper mill in Australia in 1868, changed to its current moniker in 1986, and embarked on series of mergers and acquisitions in the new millennium, the two most significant of which were its $2.4 billion acquisition of assets from Rio Tinto’s (RIO) Alcan unit in 2010 and its $5.2 billion merger with Bemis in 2019, the latter of which provided it with an NYSE listing. The stock trades around nine bucks a share, translating to an approximate market cap of $13 billion.

The company operates on a fiscal year (FY) ending June 30th. For the avoidance of doubt, the period ending June 30, 2025 is FY 2025.

Operating Segments

Currently, Amcor views its performance through two operating segments: Flexibles and Rigid Packaging.

Flexibles consists of the manufacturing of flexible and film packaging for the food and beverage, medical and pharmaceutical, fresh produce, snack food, and personal care products (amongst others). It employs ~35,000 at 160 manufacturing and support facilities in 36 countries, generating FY 2024 Adj. EBIT of $1.40 billion on revenue of $10.33 billion, representing 76% of the company’s net sales. These figures were down 2% and 7% from FY 2023 (respectively) as the unit realized negative impacts from the pass-through of lower raw material costs and sales volumes down 4%, slightly offset by favorable currency translations.

As the name suggests, Rigid Packaging provides rigid containers predominantly for food and beverage products and personal care items, as well as plastic caps for a plethora of applications. It employs ~5,000 at 52 manufacturing and support sites in 11 countries, accounting for FY 2024 Adj. EBIT of $259 million on net sales of $3.31 billion, representing declines of 2% and 7% from FY 2023. Like the percentage declines, the story was somewhat the same as with the Flexibles segment, although the drop in sales volumes was more pronounced at 8%.

Adding in corporate overhead, Amcor produced FY 2024 earnings of $0.70 per share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBIT of $1.56 billion on net sales of $13.64 billion versus $0.73 per share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBIT of $1.61 billion on net sales of $14.69 billion, representing declines of 4%, 3%, and 7%, respectively. That performance was below the company’s ten year (FY 2014-FY 2024) averages that reflected growth rates of 7%, 5%, and 1% (on a comparable constant currency basis).respectively.

Berry Global Acquisition

In a typical year, Amcor would dedicate approximately one-third of its operating cash flow to capital expenditures, about half to dividends, and the balance to acquisitions or share repurchases. That model will be modified somewhat based on its proposed acquisition of Berry Global, a maker of differentiated yet complementary – as far as Amcor is concerned – packaging products in an all-stock transaction. Announced in November 2024, stakeholders in the acquiree will receive 7.25 shares of AMCR for every one share of BERY. In addition to geography, one example of the differentiated yet complementary businesses provided by management was a yogurt cup featuring Amcor’s rigid packaging with Berry’s foil top affixed to it. Additionally, the target had just spun off its health, hygiene and specialties global nonwovens and film business that was then merged with Glatfelter to create the world’s largest nonwovens concern dubbed Magnera (MAGN) two and half weeks prior to the Amcor pronouncement.

Based on TTM (September 30, 2024) metrics and accounting for its recent spin off, Berry is contributing EBIT of $1.7 billion on net sales of $10.1 million, bringing the combination total to EBIT of $3.7 billion on net sales of $23.6 billion before giving effect to anticipated synergies. As for those synergies, management is forecasting the merger to produce $650 million per annum of cost and procurement pricing savings by the end of year three with a one-time $280 million cash benefit from working capital efficiencies that will offset costs to achieve said synergies. As a result, the deal is expected to deliver Adj. cash EPS accretion of more than 35% versus Amcor’s TTM as of September 30, 2024.

Amcor’s acquisition of Berry is anticipated to close in mid-2025.

Share Price Performance

Given the all-stock nature of the deal, risk arbitrageurs have kept a lid on Amcor’s stock price, shorting close to 100 million shares – a position that could rise as the deal closing nears. As such, shares of AMCR have traded in a range of $9.29 to $10.71 since the proposed merger’s announcement. That said, Amcor’s stock has been relatively range-bound since shortly after the pandemic selloff, trading between $8.45 and $13.61 a share since May 2020, with the preponderance of that time spent between $9 and $12 a share.

Q2 FY 2025 Financials and FY 2025 Outlook

Part of the reason for the range-bound nature of its stock price is its low growth profile and the relative predictability of the company’s earnings, with 88% of its net sales derived from non-cyclical verticals such as food, beverage, healthcare, and personal care. Management has only had to modify its annual earnings and Adj. free cash flow outlooks twice in the last half decade: a better FY 2021 emerging from the pandemic; and a worse FY 2023 with inflation impacting margins and demand. Before it announced that it was tying the knot with Berry, Amcor guided the Street to FY 2025 non-GAAP EPS of $0.74 and Adj. free cash flow of $950 million, based on range midpoints.

With the merger not expected to consummate until mid-2025, those numbers were not altered when the company reported Q2 FY 2025 financials on February 4, 2025, posting earnings of $0.161 per share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBIT of $363 million on net sales of $3.24 billion, versus $0.157 and Adj. EBIT of $352 million on net sales of $3.25 billion in Q2 FY 2024, representing (on a comparable constant currency basis) year-over-year improvements of 5% at the bottom of an Adj. EBIT lines (up 1% at the top line) as margins improved.

With an in-line bottom line versus Street expectations buttressed by positive comments about growing visibility regarding synergies in the Berry acquisition, shares of AMCR rallied 5% in the subsequent trading session to $10.05.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary

Given its proclivity to employ stock as currency in major acquisitions, Amcor’s balance sheet is not overleveraged, reflecting investment-grade net debt of $6.11 billion as of December 31, 2024 for net leverage of 3.3. With its forecasted cash flows in H2 FY 2025, that metric is expected to drop to 3.0 (or below) by June 30, 2025. Assuming the onboarding of Berry, the company anticipates deploying its ~$3 billion plus of annual operating cash flow somewhat equally amongst capital expenditures, dividends, and acquisitions/share repurchases.

Amcor’s dividend is of note, since it is an S&P 500 dividend aristocrat, meaning that it has raised its dividend every year for at least 25 years, dating back to its listing on the Australian Securities Exchange. Its current quarterly disbursement of $0.1275 equates to a yield of 5.7%

Since the announcement of the Berry deal, Street analysts have warmed to the otherwise stagnate company, currently featuring five buy and outperform ratings against three holds and a mean price objective of $11.50 as compared to only one buy against seven holds and one underperform with a mean price objective of $10.50 in the month prior to the Berry merger announcement. On average – and not giving effect to Berry – they expect Amcor to earn $0.74 a share (non-GAAP) on net sales of $13.8 billion in FY 2025, followed by $0.79 a share (non-GAAP) on net sales of $14.04 billion in FY 2026.

Insiders at the company are in agreement with the Street’s change of heart, with CEO Peter Konieczny purchasing 100,000 shares at an average price of $10.05 and board member Arun Nayar buying 100,000 shares at $9.95, both after its Q2 FY 2025 financial report.

Verdict

With its approaching combination expected to deliver solid cash EPS accretion, Amcor would make a solid covered call candidate, as it will likely be range-bound until the consummation of the Berry transaction, has a solid shot of rising after the risk arbitrageur pressure disappears post-close, and pays a solid and safe 5.7% dividend. The problem, unfortunately, is there is little liquidity in the options against the equity. This makes a covered call strategy non-viable. If it wasn't for current global trade uncertainty, I would take a small equity position in AMCR. However, given how uncertain and evolving tariff policies currently are, I am passing on any investment recommendation around Amcor.

data.title: Cummins: Tariff Turbulence Creates A Buying Opportunity (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

I’m a big fan of Cummins Inc (NYSE:CMI). I first recommended the stock in September last year as a ‘buy’ noting that the company was probably among the most underappreciated ‘compounders’ on the market as a steady business that’s been resilient throughout most business cycles. While it’s end markets have some cyclicality, Cummins was and still is poised to benefit from emissions regulations and increasing demand for electrification and alternative energy solutions. However, the stock quickly shot up 23% in December, prompting me to downgrade the stock to a ‘hold’, advising prospective investors to wait for a better opportunity before taking a position.

That seems to have been the correct call (at least so far) as shares have tumbled 28% since then given tariffs and the broader macro situation. While some of that’s attributable to the S&P 500 falling 16% over the same period, Cummins decline has been far steeper and rapid. In this article, I’ll explain why I think it’s time to get back into the stock and take a long position.

Recent Results

While I think most of Cummins’ latest decline can be attributed to the current geopolitical climate and recession fears, I think it’s helpful to put this into context with how the business has been performing up to this point. In the company’s latest quarterly results, Cummins wrapped up 2024 with $8.4 billion of revenues in the quarter, which was a slight 1% decline from last year. Most of this was due to lower North America heavy-duty and pickup truck volumes alongside the impact of the Atmus Filtration Technologies separation. That said, these headwinds were largely offset by strong demand in global power generation markets and a thriving aftermarket business coupled with improved pricing.

For the full year, sales hit a record $34.1 billion which was essentially flat compared to 2023 despite challenges in the second half from softening North America truck demand and the Atmus divestiture. On the bottom line, EBITDA soared despite flattish growth with $6.3 billion in EBITDA – good for a 18.6% margin mostly thanks to the Power Systems segment.

When we look at Q4’24 in particular, I think the story continues to be on margins and improved profitability. As I pointed out in December, Cummins was poised to benefit from favorable pricing and operational improvements and they continued to execute on this in Q4. For example, margins showed notable improvement with EBITDA reaching $1.0 billion or 12.1% of sales, a significant turnaround from the $878 million loss reported a year ago which was heavily impacted by a $2 billion regulatory settlement. Even adjusting for charges tied to the Accelera reorganization EBITDA was $1.3 billion with the EBITDA margin of 15.8% 140bps higher than last year.

Speaking of Accelera, the reorganization of the the business with $312 million in charges reflects a proactive effort by management to streamline operations and position this zero-emission segment for long-term success amid slower adoption rates. To me, I think this was critical as the X15N natural gas engine is now in full production at the Jamestown Engine Plant and gained traction with major North American fleets seeking lower emissions.

Regarding the company’s capital allocation approach, Cummins returned nearly $1 billion to shareholders through dividends while prioritizing debt reduction over share repurchases. The Atmus separation reduced shares outstanding by 5.6 million (enhancing per share metrics without direct buybacks). On capex, the company spent $1.2 billion however most of this went towards supporting investments in the HELM engine platform and capacity expansions like the $200 million commitment to power generation facilities across the U.S., UK, and India. Given that these are mostly growth capex investments as opposed to maintenance capex, I don’t think that detracts from the company’s true cash flow.

In just Q4 alone, the company generated $1.4 billion in operating cash flow and the balance sheet remains healthy with debt to capital of 38.4% and net debt to capital of 26.1%. With total debt of $4.8 billion against EBITDA of $6.3 billion, leverage is around 0.8x which is on the low side for companies in the space. Given long-term issuer debt is investment grade rated (A by S&P and A2 by Moody’s, both with stable outlook), the company can access debt capital markets with a low cost of capital. Also, Cummins’ maturities are long-term in nature and laddered, minimizing refinancing risk.

Outlook

While the recent tariff talks and recession fears might change the outlook, Cummins' management team put together a forecast for 2025 that seems cautiously optimistic. Guidance provided on the earnings conference call post quarter was for full year revenues to range from down 2% to up 3% compared to 2024 which feels like a pragmatic stance given the mixed signals across their key markets. Management guided EBITDA to land between 16.2% and 17.2% of sales, an improvement over the 15.7% figure from 2024. To me, this suggests confidence in squeezing more profitability out of a potentially flat topline driven by operational efficiencies and pricing power. The guidance reflects a tale of two halves with weaker demand expected in North America on-highway truck markets during the first half, offset by resilience in power generation and aftermarket strength throughout the year. So I see this as a balanced view acknowledging near-term softness.

In terms of the segment-level outlook, management is forecasting the Engine business to see revenues between down 2% and up 3% with EBITDA margins ticking up to 14.2% to 15.2%. I think this hints at a short-term dip from North American truck weakness but I believe the long-term story here hinges on the HELM platform’s adoption especially the natural gas variant which could gain traction as fuel economics and regulations shift. Power Systems on the other hand is expected to grow revenues by 2% to 7% with margins climbing to 19% to 20% fueled by sustained data center demand. In my view, this segment’s momentum could stretch well beyond 2025 if infrastructure investments keep pace with tech-driven power needs.

For Accelera, the revenue guidance is for $400 million to $450 million with losses narrowing to $385 million to $415 million. This shows a measured approach to zero-emission investments which I think is wise given the uneven adoption curve. Given headwinds to the Accelera business, management believes it is not currently on track to reach breakeven by 2027, its longtime goal for the segment. In light of a weaker demand outlook and efforts to reduce losses, management continues to pace investments within the segment. The reorganization following a strategic review led to streamlining of the business and a $312M million charge in 4Q24 (of which $305 million was non-cash). Within Accelera, the outlook for battery electric vehicles is favorable for commercial and industrial applications and CMI is well-positioned to capture the market given its battery cell JV (Amplify Cell Technologies), and as such, investments continue in this area. For electrolyzers, investments continue, albeit paced according to slowing customer demand and uncertainty around incentives. Meanwhile, management is being selective in fuel cells and other technologies where adoption continues to push out.

Short-term, I think Q1'25 is likely to be the low point of the year. Cummins' HD truck market build forecast embeds a stronger second half on anticipated economic recovery and pre-buy. Meanwhile, management expects growth in power generation throughout the year. Power Systems growth will primarily be driven by capacity ramp up and continued sales of larger engines in the data center market, along with pricing. It is slightly ahead of schedule in doubling its capacity for large engines by the end of this year. Separately, the parts business is healthy, growing in line with the economy along with some pricing. Distribution continues to do well, and other markets apart from power generation such as mining and oil & gas are expected to recover in the future so I think the tone is overall positive as we look past next quarter.

Longer term, I think there's still a lot of reasons to be optimistic. Through the company's 'Destination Zero' strategy, the company is aligned on an evolving global push towards decarbonization while leveraging their established dominance in diesel engines. The Power Systems segment’s record profitability tied to surging data center demand taps into a secular growth driver fueled by the exponential rise in cloud computing AI and digital infrastructure; trends that are projected to drive global data center power needs up by 160% by 2030. To me, this exposure to a durable long-term demand wave underpinned by tech giants like Amazon (AMZN) and Google (GOOG) needing reliable power generation offers Cummins a clear runway for sustained earnings growth. Beyond that, Cummins' cash generation and strong balance sheet equips them well to navigate any potential macroeconomic headwinds. I'll discuss more of these risks in the next section.

Risks

There are three risks I think are worth pointing out that investors should be aware of when thinking about Cummins' business. The first risk I see is the softening demand in the North America on-highway truck market which management has highlighted as a drag on the first half of 2025. Heavy-duty truck production is projected to range from flat to down 10% and medium-duty down 5% to 15% year over year. If this weakness persists or the expected second-half prebuy tied to EPA27 regulations fails to kick in, revenues and margins in the Engine segment (a cornerstone of earnings) could take a hit.

Another risk would be the slower adoption of zero-emission technologies in the Accelera business. With a $300+ million reorganization charge in Q4 and management conceding that breakeven by 2027 is off track, I worry that prolonged delays in customer uptake or shifting regulatory support could drain cash flow and divert focus from the profitable core business. There's obviously execution risk here that could weigh on performance but this isn't a major driver of revenues and earnings and so the risk here is to the upside if things work out better than expected, in my view.

Lastly, the third risk I’m focused on is the potential impact of tariffs which have resurfaced as a concern with recent developments in U.S. trade policy. In reports from outlets like Reuters and Bloomberg, there have been speculation that the Trump administration is likely to place tariffs as high as 25% on imports from Canada and Mexico. These are key markets for Cummins and as an international company, it's quite clear that business in the short term is going to be impacted. That said, on the earnings call, CFO Mark Smith noted that “to the extent that [they] do incur [tariffs] then [they] think it’s important that the market feels those and [they'll] look to pass those on” suggests confidence in pricing power but I’m skeptical this can fully offset margin erosion if costs spike.

Beyond direct impacts tariffs could ripple through the broader economy potentially triggering inflation or a recession as warned by economists. For Cummins, this could dampen demand for trucks and power generation equipment as customers tighten budgets. In my view, this tariff risk, while not an immediate knockout punch, adds an element of risk that a lot of investors might not be comfortable with. But as I'll discuss in the valuation section, I think this risk is largely baked into the current price.

Valuation and Wrap Up

Cummins is relatively underappreciated by the sellside, even after strong 2024 results. At present, there are currently seven ‘buy’ ratings, 14 ‘hold’ ratings, and two ‘sell’ ratings. From the current price to the average price target one year out this implies about 35.4% upside excluding the dividend. However, I do expect that if sentiment shifts around the economy and businesses pullback spending, it wouldn’t surprise me to see a few cuts to target prices after Q1’25 results.

At the current price through, I think a lot of bad news has already been priced in. For 12.0x P/E and 7.5x EV/EBITDA, I think investors are getting a pretty good deal considering just how expensive Cummins was three months ago (source: Bloomberg). While 2025 and maybe even 2026 might be a bit challenging as the company works through a more challenging macro environment, this seems to be baked into the share price. Also, Cummins trades inexpensively to peers that trade at an average multiple of 10.9x P/E.

Ultimately, while shares have taken a beating, I see value in today's prices which is why I'm shifting my stance back to ‘buy’ from ‘hold.’ The company’s Power Systems strength and alignment with long-term trends like decarbonization and data center growth are long-term positives that I think will outweigh short-term tariff and recession concerns. While there could be some doubt that 2025 numbers aren't met because of near-term disruptions, Cummins has the balance sheet to support future growth and as was put by CEO Jennifer Rumsey, “the slower and messier this goes [their] view is the better it is strategically for Cummins” as one of the leaders in the industry. At 12.0x P/E and 7.5x EV/EBITDA, I see a bargain for a proven compounder with risks already baked in offering a strong entry point for the long haul.
